The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Plaintiff Raymond DiGiacomo Jr.'s motion to impose issue, evidentiary, and monetary sanctions against defendant Denise Williams is denied. Williams was ordered on December 9, 2025, to provide objection-free responses to special interrogatory nos. 26, 27, 29, 30, 35, 36, and 39. Williams's responses contain no objections and comply with the Code. Generally, Williams responds that she does not recall the circumstances of her statement in a prior litigation that HealthRight 360 received certain grievances submitted by DiGiacomo, and she provides the identities of people with information about that assertion (herself and her attorney). Her responses are sufficient and comply with this court's prior order.

The court exercises its discretion to consider defendant's late-filed opposition brief.

The court concludes that the motion lacked substantial justification and orders plaintiff to pay $100 to defendant within 30 days.

1 - PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO Motion For Sanctions Against Defendants, Joseph G. Helfrick, And Lauria Tokunaga Gates And Linn, Llp.

Plaintiff's motion for sanctions is denied. The court considers defendants' late opposition. Plaintiff was not prejudiced and filed a reply on the merits. Plaintiff fails to demonstrate that defendants' counsel acted frivolously within the meaning of CCP 128.5(b)(2) ["totally and completely without merit"] or CCP 128.7. The record shows that defense counsel extensively met and conferred with plaintiff regarding the original answer, agreed to file an amended answer, and indicated that other changes would not be made. (Helfrick Decl., pars. 4-6.) While defense counsel should have responded to plaintiff's requests to meet and confer regarding the amended answer (CCP 430.41(a) [obligation to meet and confer again where there is an amended pleading]), the court cannot say that counsels' failure to do so was "frivolous" or in bad faith to harass plaintiff.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the court's ruling and must drop a hard courtesy copy in Dept. 302's inbox.

2 - PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO's Motion For Order Compelling Defendant Denise Williams To Serve Answers Without Objections To Srogs2 And Request For Monetary Sanction.

Plaintiff's motion to compel responses to the special interrogatories (set two) ("SROGS") propounded on defendant Williams is granted. The court considers defendant's late opposition.

Plaintiff e-served the SROGS on September 20, 2025. (DiGiacomo Decl., par. 2.) The responses were due 30 days later (October 20, 2025). (CCP 2030.260(a).) But since the documents were e-served the deadline was extended two court days (October 22, 2025.). (CCP 1010.6(a)(3)(B).) Here, defendant served her untimely response on October 24, 2025. (Helfrick Decl., par. 6; DiGiacomo Decl., Ex. 6.) Because the response was untimely, defendant waived all objections. (CCP 2030.290(a). Defendant also failed to file a "motion" for relief from this waiver. (Id.)

Therefore, the court orders defendant to provide objection free substantive responses to the SROGS within 15 days of notice of entry of this order. Defendant did not act with substantial justification and the court awards sanctions of $7 payable from defendant Williams or attorney Helfrick.

Order is signed.

PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO'S Motion For Order Compelling Defendant Denise Williams To Further Respond To Dfp1 And Request For Monetary Sanction.

Plaintiff Raymond Vincent DiGiacomo, Jr.'s motion for an order requiring defendant Denise Williams to further respond to his first request for production of documents is denied.

DiGiacomo's RFP No. 1 requests all documents identified by Williams in response to Form Interrogatory 17.1(d). Williams responded that no documents were identified. (DiGiacomo Decl., Ex. 3; see also id., Ex. 5 [form interrogatory responses].) This response is full and complete. Williams is not relying on documents for her denial of DiGiacomo's requests for admission. No further response is required. Ordinarily a party would be required to report whether documents previously existed and were destroyed, but this RFP is in the nature of contention discovery that ascertains what a party is relying on (and might introduce at trial) in support of a particular litigating position. The court concludes these responses substantially comply with the relevant statutes.

DiGiacomo also requests an order requiring Williams to replace her electronically signed verification with a wet signature. The court denies this request for the reasons stated in its prior discovery order: electronic signatures are as binding as ink signatures where a party intends them to be, and the fact that Williams placed an electronic signature on a verification sufficiently indicates her intent to be bound. (See Civ. Code, sections 1633.7 & 1633.9.)

The court denies sanctions, concluding that DiGiacomo's position with respect to the content of the response had substantial justification in Code of Civil Procedure, section 2031.230, and his continued insistence on a wet signature predated the court's previous order rejecting this request.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling in complaince with CRC3.1312

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Compelling Defendant Denise Williams To Further Respond To Rfa1 And Request For Monetary Sanction; Declaration; And, Memorandum.
Off Calendar Oct-15-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Plaintiff Raymond Vincent DiGiacomo Jr., in pro per.
Atty. Joseph Helfrick, Esq. of Lauria Tokunaga Gates & Linn, LLP (tel: 916-492-2000) for Defendants Healthright 360, Denise Williams and Vitka Eisen.

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO's Motion For Order Compelling Defendant Denise Williams To Further Respond To Rfa1 And Request For Monetary Sanction.

Matter discussed. Plaintiff objects to the matter being recorded and requests a court reporter pursuant to Government Code section 69957(a). The Court denies the request for court reporter and proceeds with the matter being reported by For The Record.

Matter argued and submitted. Upon conclusion, the Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Raymond Vincent DiGiacomo, Jr.'s motion to compel defendant Denise Williams to serve further responses to his requests for admission, nos. 1-7, and for a wet signature, is denied.

With respect to the content of Williams's responses, Williams stated that she had insufficient information to know whether she (or someone acting on her behalf) received the medical grievance at issue. For those RFAs asking her to admit receipt (nos. 1-2), she stated that she could not admit or deny the request because she lacked information about receipt. For those RFAs asking her to admit facts about what happened to the grievance after she received it (nos. 3-7) Williams restated that she lacked information about receipt and on that basis denied the RFAs.

These responses comply with Code of Civil Procedure, section 2033.220, and are proper. "Requests for admissions . . . are primarily aimed at setting at rest a triable issue so that it will not have to be tried." (Cembrook v. Superior Court (1961) 56 Cal.2d 423, 429.) Williams's verified responses are sufficiently clear about what information she possesses and the basis for her denial. DiGiacomo contends that Williams's investigation of the facts underlying the RFAs is insufficiently diligent but this amounts to speculation and does not give rise to a duty to amend.

The court also denies the request for a wet signature. Electronic signatures are valid where a party intends the application of an electronic signature to have the same legal effect as an ink signature. (See Civ. Code, sections 1633.7, 1633.9.) DiGiacomo raises no basis sufficient for the court to doubt that Williams intended her signature to be binding.

Both parties seek sanctions. "The court shall impose a monetary sanction . . . against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel further response, unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." (Code Civ. Proc., section 2033.290, subd. (d).) The court concludes that DiGiacomo's motion lacks substantial justification but reduces the sanction in the exercise of its discretion. DiGiacomo shall pay $500 to Williams within 30 days of entry of this order. DiGiacomo is cautioned that future meritless discovery motions may subject him to increased sanctions.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling, with a cover letter demonstrating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Recorded by For the Record. (301/CVA)
